122.metadata_frame: The poem in the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
122.main_question:
Identify the poetic form of the following work, titled "To Time."
ON some grave afternoon, in years to be,
When I pass down the withering country walks,
Will he be but the scent blown out the stalks
Of the wrecked rose, and nothing more to me?
And I that was?-this wild girl that am I -
But a blurred figure fleeting by the pane?— 
I that do eat my heart out, and am fain
To cast myself down on the clods and die?-
But scent, but mist? I will not have it so.
Set us twain fast in some far weather's round;
Press not for me that wine from vale or hill,
Who drink, forgetting youth and yester go.
You dare not. I would dash the cup to ground.
Hurt me. That I loved once, would I love still.

126.main_question:
What is the rhyme scheme in the sestet of the following sonnet?

Lawrence, of virtuous father virtuous son,
Now that the fields are dank, and ways are mire,
Where shall we sometimes meet, and by the fire
Help waste a sullen day, what may be won
From the hard season gaining? Time will run
On smoother, till Favonius reinspire
The frozen earth, and clothe in fresh attire
The lily and rose that neither sowed nor spun.
What neat repast shall feast us, light and choice,
Of Attic taste, with wine, whence we may rise
To hear the lute well touched, or artful voice
Warble immortal notes and Tuscan air?
He who of those delights can judge, and spare
To interpose them oft, is not unwise.

The contraction of the right atrium draws blood into the cavity of that chamber from the vena cava, and the contraction of the left atrium draws blood into the cavity of that chamber from the pulmonary vein.

514.ground_truth: It can hardly be said to be obvious why, in uncultured races or individuals, there should arise that invariable tendency to represent natural forces as conscious and anthropomorphic. It is not easy to say. It is probable that it arose out of the primitive belief in the soul, which was common to all races of men. The savage, when he saw the body of his friend or kinsman lying dead, felt that the soul had left it, and that it was now a mere corpse. But he could not believe that the soul was gone for ever. He thought that it had gone away to some place where it would remain for ever, and that it might be recalled by certain ceremonies. I was not a foreigner in Japan, but I was a foreigner in China. I had been in Japan for some time, and I had become so accustomed to the Japanese way of doing things that I was almost Japanese myself. I had learned to speak Japanese, and I had learned to think in Japanese. I had become so much a part of the Japanese people that I was almost one of them. But when I went into China, I was a foreigner again. I was no longer one of them. I was a stranger in a strange land. I was no longer at home. I was no longer comfortable. I was no longer happy. I was no longer myself. I was no longer a part of the Chinese people. I was no longer one of them.

707.ground_truth: At present, travel to the moon is impossible. But perhaps some centuries to come the problem will be solved. Before the invention of balloons and aeroplanes, after all, no one thought seriously of being able to rise in the air. Whoever finds out a means of suppressing or even diminishing the force of gravitation, that mysterious power which holds the heavenly bodies together and keeps us fixed to the earth, will thus enable men to leave their planet and launch out upon the conquest of neighbouring worlds. And the Moon may be the first stage of these inter-planetary voyages.

708.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbe Moreux, first published in 1912.
708.main_question: In a short paragraph, vividly describe the passage from night to day on the Moon.
708.ground_truth: It is still night in the region in which we have landed on the surface of the Moon. The sky sparkles with incomparable splendour. The eye can distinguish in it thousands and thousands of stars. Not even the glories of a tropical night can give any idea of what night on the Moon must be. But soon the Sun will appear. There is no dawn to herald it, but already there is rising in the east a long, spindle-shaped trail of whiteness; it is the zodiacal light, ten times more brilliant than with us. For hours this false dawn rises higher in the sky; its tints of phosphorescent colours are brighter and brighter from the summit to the base; in the midst of the enormous cone, all aglow with green and opal, there gleams a resplendent star: it is the beautiful Venus, hardly recognisable amid its mantle of light. But already the fine rays of the Sun's corona are heralding the day-star.
708.length_spec: a short paragraph, about 100–150 words total
708.answer: 1000 feet above the level of the sea, the sky was clear and cloudless. The sun had just risen, and its rays were already beginning to warm the surface of the moon. 709.ground_truth: It is likely that there are in the Moon some small craters formed in this way. But the volcanic theory of the lunar circles does not explain everything. One might, first of all, object that it is very strange that we generally do not find in the interior of these great circles any serious inequalities of the ground, any mountain resembling those of the outer rampart. One would expect to find regular concentric rings at various distances from the centre, marking the progressive decrease of the eruptive phenomena in the course of geological time. But there is nothing of the kind!

709.answer: 1873, p. 453). The crater is surrounded by a high wall, which is not always continuous, but is often broken into detached portions. The floor of the crater is generally covered with a layer of fine dust, which is easily stirred up by the wind, and is often seen floating in the air above the crater. The crater is usually filled with water, which is supplied by the melting snows of the mountains. The water is very pure, and is often used by the natives for drinking purposes. The crater is about 1 mile in diameter, and is about 100 feet deep. The temperature of the water is about 32° Fahr., and it is said to be very refreshing. 710.main_question: Why are children troublesome, or, as we crudely and falsely term it, "bad"? Answer in a short paragraph.
710.ground_truth: Usually, from one of two reasons. They lack vitality, or they overflow with uncontrolled vitality. Fretfulness, laziness, cowardice, lying, and even aggressive faults like perversity and obstinacy, are often due to lack of vitality—a pathetic, misjudged protest against being forced into the wrong work. Bad temper, cruelty, roughness, stealing, and all the myriad acts we classify as "mischievous," may result from the great gift of superabundant vitality—an energy which, like electricity, is capable of service, but disastrous when uncontrolled.
710.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 70–90 words total
710.answer:
99 

Children are troublesome because they are not yet able to control themselves. They have not learned to do what is right, and they have not learned to do what is wrong. They have not learned to think, and they have not learned to feel. They have not learned to love, and they have not learned to hate. They have not learned to be good, and they have not learned to be bad. They are not yet able to distinguish between right and wrong, between good and bad. They are not yet able to control their own actions. They are not yet able to govern themselves. They are not yet able to restrain themselves. They are not yet able to obey the laws of society. They are not yet able to obey the laws of nature. They are not yet able to obey the laws of God. They are not yet able to obey the laws of their own conscience.

CHAPTER VII THE INDUS 

THE Indus River, which rises in the Himalayas, flows through the Punjab and empties into the Arabian Sea. It is the longest river in India, and its valley is the most fertile part of the country. 712.metadata_frame: This question requests information from an American encyclopedia published in 1897; the best answer will reflect the knowledge and mode of exposition of that era.
712.ground_truth: A French writer of comedies and pseudo-scientific romances. His first comedy, Pailles Rompues, was produced 1850; it was followed by Onze Jours de Siège; L'Oncle d'Amérique; and several comic operas. These had great success in Paris; but Verne's fame rests chiefly on his long series of mock-scientific extravaganzas, beginning with Five Weeks in a Balloon, 1863. More than 30 have been translated into English and published in large editions in England and the United States.

Mechanical science, thus early and very rapidly progressing, soon suggested of itself the thought that nature was all one vast mechanism. The philosophers, with their love of grand generalizations, easily seized upon this idea. They tried to expound it, to reflect upon it, to defend it, to develop its meaning.
